claim.hypothesis: We expected Covid-19 humor to be more aversive than humor on more benign topics.
claim.statement: Differences in Aversiveness were found (+0.39, t=+2.26, p<.05) between Covid Verbal (M=0.87) and NonCovid Verbal (M=0.48).

data.access_details: The material used in the current study, as well as the dataset and the R script for the analyses, are available in the Open Science Framework repository (https://osf.io/gj52h/).
data.notes: The survey was run by the authors, however, they extracted the jokes from Facebook. Results are based on a sample of 1751 native Italian speakers.

method.description: They use a “hierarchical” approach with four models (for each dependent variable) of increasing complexity were built and compared in terms of goodness of fit, based on Linear Mixed Models with crossed random effects.
method.steps: (1) Extracted the jokes and likeability of the jokes from Facebook; (2) Constructed the survey using LimeSurvey; (3) Ran the survey from March 18-March 30, 2020 in Italy using posts and targeted advertising on Facebook; (4) Used linear mixed models to conduct statistical analysis on the relationship between type of humor, demographic factors, personality measures, and psychological distance on the outcome variables of funniness and aversiveness.
method.control_variables: crossed random effects for Participants, Items, and Province of completion

results.summary: Differences in Aversiveness were found (+0.39, t=+2.26, p<.05) between Covid Verbal (M=0.87) and NonCovid Verbal (M=0.48).

metadata.original_paper_title: Funny but aversive: A large-scale survey on the emotional response to Covid-19 humor in the Italian population during the lockdown
